nutritionindianfhs-5

Where a Child Lives

In India, where a child lives shapes their chance of being stunted almost as much as how poor their family is.

Nehchal Singh·2026·6 min

Stunting — a child too short for their age — is the clearest single marker of early-life deprivation. It reflects not one bad month but years of inadequate nutrition, repeated illness, and the conditions a child is born into. Across India, more than a third of children under five are stunted, but that national figure hides enormous variation: the map above runs from states where roughly one child in six is affected to states where it is closer to one in two.

The instinct is to explain that variation with poverty — and poverty is certainly part of it. But it is not the whole story, and the rest of this piece is about what the map of poverty alone leaves unexplained.

A third of India's children are stunted — but where you are born changes that chance dramatically.

02Income explains part of it — but only part

If prosperity were the whole answer, richer states would have less stunting in a tidy straight line. They mostly do trend that way. But the scatter around that line is wide: at the same income level, some states do markedly better than others, and a few poorer states outperform richer ones outright. Income sets a tendency, not a destiny.

The states sitting well below the line are the interesting ones: they have less stunting than their income would predict. The states above it carry more. That vertical distance — what a state does relative to its economic hand — is where the real questions begin.

03Within states, poverty bites unevenly

Zoom inside each state and a second pattern appears. The gap between the poorest and richest children is not the same everywhere. In some states a poor child faces a far higher stunting risk than a rich one in the same state; in others the gap is narrow. Being poor is more punishing for a child's growth in some places than in others.

how rich and poor are defined

"Poorest" and "richest" here are the bottom and top fifths of households on NFHS's asset-based wealth index — built from what a household owns and its housing and sanitation, ranked across all of India. It measures long-run living standards, not this year's income.

04And it isn't only about poverty

The final piece is the one that unsettles the simple story. Even among India's richest children — the top fifth of households — stunting does not disappear. In several states it remains strikingly common, at rates a wealthy country would find alarming. Whatever drives stunting in India, it reaches beyond household poverty into something more structural: diet, sanitation, maternal health, and the conditions of a place itself.

Put together, the four maps tell a single story. Stunting is widespread and unevenly spread; income explains some of that unevenness but leaves much unexplained; the penalty of poverty varies sharply from state to state; and even the richest children are not spared. Where a child lives — the state, its institutions, its sanitation and health systems — turns out to matter nearly as much as how poor their family is.

That is a description, not a diagnosis. These maps show associations, not causes; a state that beats its income could be doing something right, or could simply differ in ways this data does not capture. But the pattern is consistent enough to be worth taking seriously — and worth the harder work of explaining.

Built with NFHS-5 (2019–21) and MOSPI state income data. Code: github.com/NehchalSingh

Maps and notes from India's public data.
